_meta.description: Datacenter Power & Cooling deep-dive (this is the 'Power & Cooling' tab). The physical layer that has become the binding constraint on AI deployment, and its cross-links into the energy supply chain (Hard Assets map).

bottleneck.title: Power & cooling — not chips — now gate the AI buildout
bottleneck.body: By 2026 datacenters are ~4% of US electricity and rising. The binding constraints have moved downstream of silicon: grid interconnect queues and multi-year transformer lead times gate where sites can be built, while rack densities of 100-130kW+ force a jump to liquid cooling (~15% of the mix in 2024 to ~76% by 2026, mandated by GB200). The coolant-distribution-unit (CDU) is the specific cooling bottleneck, with the market scaling ~$1B → ~$7.7B. This is where the AI hardware chain meets the energy supply chain.

chain.tick: ETN, GEV, Hitachi Energy, Siemens Energy ENR.DE
chain.det: Large power transformers and switchgear have 2-4yr lead times and are the gating item for new sites. Grid interconnect queues now stretch years in key US/EU markets. Cross-link to the copper/grid chain in the Hard Assets map.

chain.tick: GEV (gas turbines), CMI, CAT; nuclear PPAs CEG
chain.det: With grid access scarce, hyperscalers turn to behind-the-meter gas turbines and nuclear PPAs/SMRs. Gas turbine slots are now booked years out. This is the deepest cross-link into the energy supply chain (gas + nuclear).
Uninterruptible power supplies + battery backup sized for GPU-density loads. Lithium BESS increasingly used for ride-through and grid services.

chain.role: Busways, PDUs, switchgear in the hall
chain.det: Distributing 100-130kW+ per rack reshapes busways and PDUs. Higher-voltage DC distribution (800VDC) is emerging to cut conversion losses at the rack.
Delta Electronics leads high-efficiency DC power supplies; AI racks drive demand for higher-density, higher-efficiency PSUs. PSU lead times stretched.
Vertical power delivery and 48V-to-point-of-load conversion for 1kW+ accelerators is a fast-evolving silicon bottleneck. Content-per-GPU rising.

Direct-to-chip liquid cooling jumps to ~76% of the mix by 2026. The CDU (coolant distribution unit) is the specific capacity bottleneck; quick-disconnects and cold plates are the high-growth components.

chain.tick: Modine MOD, Johnson Controls JCI, Trane TT
chain.det: Chillers, dry coolers and (where water allows) evaporative systems reject the heat. Water availability is becoming a siting constraint — cross-link to the water chain in the Hard Assets map.

intersections.body: 24/7 carbon-free baseload via PPAs and SMRs is the premium AI power solution; the CEG-Microsoft Three Mile Island restart is the template. Cross-link to the nuclear chain in the Hard Assets map.
intersections.demand: Hyperscalers contracting 20yr PPAs; SMR pipeline building

intersections.body: Gas-fired CCGT and behind-the-meter turbines are the default fast, dispatchable solution where the grid can't deliver. Cross-link to the gas/LNG chain.
intersections.demand: Gas turbine order books booked years out

intersections.body: Every MW of AI load needs transformers, switchgear and copper. Simultaneous demand from AI + EVs + grid hardening. Cross-link to the copper chain.
Evaporative cooling and chip cooling raise water demand; arid-region siting (AZ/TX) is increasingly water-constrained. Cross-link to the water chain.
intersections.demand: Water availability now a siting filter

ai_themes.body: GB200 NVL72 and successors push rack power an order of magnitude above legacy ~10-15kW racks, breaking air cooling and conventional distribution.
Direct-to-chip liquid cooling goes from niche (~15% in 2024) to majority (~76% by 2026). Cold plates, CDUs and quick-disconnects are the highest-growth components.
Higher-voltage DC distribution to cut conversion losses and copper at extreme rack densities — Nvidia is pushing the ecosystem toward 800VDC.
The scarcest input is now interconnected power, not chips or capital — driving onsite generation, nuclear PPAs and grid-edge investment.

cycle.body: At the inflection from niche to standard; cold plates and CDUs are badly under-built vs the GB200-era ramp. Highest-torque sub-theme, with cooling-standard fragmentation as the main risk.
Content-per-GPU rising with 1kW+ accelerators and 48V/800VDC transitions. Delta and the VRM names ride density up; tied to the broader capex cycle.
Gas turbine and nuclear PPA demand is structural and supply-constrained (order books booked out). Cross-link to the energy supply chain; the deepest non-semis exposure.
Transformers and switchgear are the gating item with multi-year lead times and simultaneous AI/EV/grid demand. Long structural runway; see the copper/grid chain in Hard Assets.
